perturbation.original_text: Dolphin agrees to complete its photo-editing services within 14 days of receiving the original digital photo files.
perturbation.changed_text: Dolphin agrees to complete its photo-editing services within 3 days of receiving the original digital photo files.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the completion time to 3 days may create an unreasonable deadline that violates labor laws regarding fair working conditions and reasonable time to complete tasks.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly setting a maximum task completion time, requiring tasks to be completed in an unreasonably short time frame (3 days) can lead to violations of minimum wage and overtime laws if employees are forced to work excessive hours without proper compensation to meet the unrealistic deadline. The Fair Labor Standards Act requires employers to pay employees at least the minimum wage for all hours worked and overtime pay for hours worked over 40 in a workweek.
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the photo-editing completion time from 14 days to 3 days, which the Perturbation Explanation suggests may create an unreasonable deadline that violates labor laws. The Law Explanation states that requiring tasks to be completed in an unreasonably short time frame (3 days) can lead to violations of minimum wage and overtime laws if employees are forced to work excessive hours without proper compensation to meet the unrealistic deadline. Since the Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition states that a contradiction exists when a contract sets a deadline or requirement that violates federal or state law, the change from 14 to 3 days introduces a deadline that has the potential to violate labor laws. GovInfo is not helpful to determine the specifics of labor law, but the explanation is.

perturbation.original_text: No action, whether in contract or tort (including negligence), or otherwise arising out of or in connection with this Agreement, may be brought by Dolphin more than six months after the cause of action has occurred.
perturbation.changed_text: No action, whether in contract or tort (including negligence), or otherwise arising out of or in connection with this Agreement, may be brought by Dolphin more than one month after the cause of action has occurred.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the statute of limitations to one month is likely an unreasonably short period and violates Nevada's statute of limitations for breach of contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: NRS 11.190 Actions which must be commenced within specified periods.
perturbation.law_explanation: Nevada Revised Statute 11.190 dictates a much longer statute of limitations for various legal claims. Specifically, for actions upon a contract, the limitation is six years. Reducing the contractual period to one month is illegal, as the State provides for a much longer period to bring suit.

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the statute of limitations for bringing an action from six months to one month. The Law Explanation states that Nevada Revised Statute 11.190 dictates a six-year statute of limitations for actions upon a contract. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing NRS 11.190 under 'ACTIONS OTHER THAN FOR THE RECOVERY OF REAL PROPERTY,' confirms that Chapter 11 covers limitation periods, suggesting the one-month limitation contradicts state law. The Perturbation Explanation also indicates this change violates Nevada's statute of limitations for breach of contract.

perturbation.original_text: DGT shall be entitled to terminate this Agreement if Dolphin fails to meet its requirements and material obligation hereunder and only after DGT have given Dolphin written notice of such failure and only after Dolphin has not rectified such failure within 10 days of the notice.
perturbation.changed_text: DGT shall be entitled to terminate this Agreement if Dolphin fails to meet its requirements and material obligation hereunder and only after DGT have given Dolphin written notice of such failure and only after Dolphin has not rectified such failure within 1 day of the notice.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period to one day is an unreasonably short time and may not allow Dolphin adequate time to rectify the failure, potentially violating principles of fairness and good faith in contract law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: NRS 104.1304 Course of performance, course of dealing and usage of trade.
perturbation.law_explanation: NRS 104.1304 speaks to the importance of course of performance in understanding contracts. While not directly mandating a specific cure period, establishing a course of performance often involves reasonable opportunities to rectify issues. A one-day cure period is likely to be considered commercially unreasonable, and thus conflict with the overall principles of good faith and fair dealing implied in contract law as interpreted through course of performance. This is even more true if a prior course of dealing had established a longer cure period.

perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the cure period from 10 days to 1 day, which the explanation argues is an unreasonably short time. The Law Explanation states that a one-day cure period is likely to be considered commercially unreasonable and may conflict with good faith. NRS 104.1304, included in Scraped Law Snippet 1, refers to the "Obligation of good faith." While NRS 104.1304 doesn't directly mandate a specific cure period, the change creates an inconsistency by potentially violating the obligation of good faith implied in contract law.
